data.title: Despite Being Bullish On Tesla, I Think TSLY Is A Yield Trap
data.description:
The YieldMax TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:TSLY) attracts a lot of interest because of its high-yielding monthly income (a nearly 80% distribution rate, according to the fund, and the most recent dividend was $0.4638/share). But honestly, it looks like a yield trap. Don't fall for it.

The way the TSLY ETF is structured — it seeks to make income from employing options strategies on Tesla (TSLA) stock — sets it up for poor total returns relative to Tesla stock.

TSLY generally underperforms greatly when TSLA stock rises, while seeing almost as much downside as TSLA when it falls. The risk/reward ratio is against you, and I'll show examples of that later on.

Moreover, the dividend is highly unstable and can continue falling over time due to NAV erosion unless TSLA stock rallies. But if you think TSLA will rally, you'd be better off buying the stock directly.

I'm actually bullish on Tesla stock, as I gave it a Buy rating last month after a big sell-off. I even mentioned a TSLA options strategy that can give you a great risk/reward ratio (unlike TSLY), so I recommend reading my bullish thesis on Tesla.

Since I'm bullish on Tesla, I expect this ETF to rise in the short term as a result. However, I simply don't like this fund's strategy. These two factors balance out, leading me to a "Hold" rating. But as I said, buying TSLA itself, either through shares or the options strategy I laid out, is much better.

How Does The TSLY ETF Work?

The fund's strategy involves several parts. First, TSLY doesn't own Tesla shares directly. Instead, it generally takes a synthetic long position in the stock. This means that it buys at-the-money (ATM) call options on TSLA and simultaneously sells ATM puts with the same strike price and expiration. It's called a synthetic long position because it basically mimics Tesla stock's performance without having to buy shares.

But mimicking Tesla's performance isn't enough, as it needs to generate income for investors. Therefore, to generate income, TSLY will either employ a covered call strategy (selling an OTM call) or a covered call credit spread strategy (selling an OTM call while buying an OTM call of the same expiration but an even higher strike, for a net credit). The OTM calls generally have expirations of 1 month or less.

When the fund thinks TSLA stock will rebound sharply, it may shift to the covered call spread strategy rather than the covered call in order to limit losses on the short call if TSLA spikes.

This lowers the income the fund collects, but the idea is to limit losses and provide extra upside.

The rest of TSLY's assets are generally held in short-term U.S. Treasuries and cash. These positions provide collateral for the options positions and also generate some extra income.

Everything I just described can be found in the screenshot I included below from the fund's prospectus.

The fund continuously rolls its options, meaning contracts are replaced as they expire.

What's Wrong With TSLY?

The bad thing about TSLY is that it underperforms TSLA significantly on the upswing, while still performing poorly on the downswing. During strong rallies, TSLY underperforms because the short calls it sells cap the upside. The synthetic long increases in value, but if TSLY sells a $260 strike call, for example, there's no more upside if TSLA goes over $260.

Meanwhile, when Tesla stock is falling, the income collected from selling calls isn't enough to offset the heavy losses — at least it hasn't been in the past.

Here's an example. The chart below starts from December 17, 2024, when TSLA stock topped. The candlesticks represent TSLY's performance, while the blue and red bars represent TSLA, and the results are adjusted for dividends. This downturn led to a 46.38% drop in TSLY and a 50.98% drop in TSLA. That's hardly any better than TSLA's performance.

The extra income generated from options selling didn't shield the downside enough, especially relative to how much upside it caps on the way up. Below is a chart of TSLY vs. TSLA on the upswing from October 11, 2024, to December 17, 2024. TSLA returned 117.99%, while TSLY returned 80.19%.

This strategy hasn't done well in the longer term, either. Since inception, TSLY has returned a dismal -12.46%, including dividends, while TSLA has gained 32.66%.

It seems to me that the fund has almost all the downside of owning TSLA stock while having capped upside. Owning it makes little sense to me.

The situation isn't much better when Tesla trades sideways. Below is a chart from June 12, 2023, to September 30, 2024, when TSLA stock returned 0.86%, but TSLY returned 6.9% after factoring in dividends.

Why Are The Dividends Falling?

People buy TSLY for its dividend, but the dividend has been falling. Its July 2023 distribution was $2.1322 per share, but the most recent one was $0.4638 per share. The dividends vary month by month, but the trend has generally been lower, even though TSLA stock has risen since TSLY's inception. Why, though?

I suspect a big reason is the fund's NAV decline. You can see how its share price keeps declining when the chart isn't adjusted for dividends.

The fund basically pays all its income as dividends, meaning it doesn't retain anything. And as its NAV drops (especially if TSLA falls), it has fewer funds to invest, and less funds invested means shrinking dividends.

Next, when Tesla's OTM calls are relatively expensive, the fund might switch to its call spread strategy rather than covered calls. That's because when calls are expensive, it can mean the market anticipates TSLA to rise. TSLY's fund managers might think the same and employ the call spread strategy to help capture some upside potential and reduce losses from the short call. However, this strategy lowers the income the fund can collect, reducing the dividend.

If Tesla's stock price stays depressed, I'm almost certain TSLY's next dividend will be depressed relative to past payouts.

Would You Be Better Off Just Buying Tesla Stock?

I think if you're bullish on Tesla, you should just buy Tesla stock. If you're not bullish on TSLA and are looking for income from this ETF, I don't think its strategy is great. You'd be better off employing your own options strategies on TSLA (if you have experience with options trading), which would give you more control.

In addition, TSLY's high payout is hard to maintain due to the factors I mentioned earlier. It's not steady income.

The Bottom Line On TSLY

TSLY is an interesting ETF in theory, but in practice, its performance has been poor. It underperformed TSLA notably in sideways and uptrending markets, while it hardly outperformed during downtrends (you'd still be down a lot). Importantly, its dividend payout keeps trending lower, and this can continue unless TSLA stock rebounds. But as I said earlier, if you think TSLA will rebound, you might as well trade TSLA and/or its options directly.

Unless you have a very specific, short-term outlook and are taking a tactical trade on TSLY, the ETF probably isn't a good choice for most investors. It looks like a yield trap to me.

data.title: JFR: Tactical Buy After The Storm (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

The Nuveen Floating Rate Income Fund (NYSE:JFR) is a fixed income closed end fund we last covered at the beginning of the year on the back of a corporate action. At the time, we warned investors that a period of weakness was coming due to the structural mechanics of a rights offering. The CEF stabilized when the corporate action ended in February 2025, but has been recently sold-off significantly on the back of 'tariff Armageddon'.

In today's article we are going to explore JFR's valuation in light of the recent market action, and outline why the CEF is oversold on a short-term basis and thus represents a tactical 'Buy' opportunity.

Analytics first

  • AUM: $1.2 billion.
  • Sharpe Ratio: 0.1 (3Y).
  • Std. Deviation: 6.7 (3Y).
  • Yield: 13.5%
  • Premium/Discount to NAV: -12%
  • Z-Stat: -4
  • Leverage Ratio: 35%.
  • Composition: Leveraged Loans

A leveraged take on floating rate loans

JFR is a CEF which focuses on floating rate loans rated below investment grade:

  • Senior Loans: 85.8%
  • Cash: 11.9%
  • Bonds: 11%
  • Other: 1%

Kindly note the CEF reports its exposures including the leverage. Also note that the rights offering was a smart move in today's environment since it allowed the CEF to increase its cash balance without incurring any loss crystallization via collateral sales.

The fund is overweight single-B names, which represent more than 54% of the fund:

  • BBB: 8.6%
  • BB: 9.5%
  • B: 54.1%
  • CCC: 4.7%
  • Not Rated: 2.8%

The fund therefore is expected to have a lower NAV and price when single-B and double-BB spreads move up. This is exactly what has happened in the past month.

Credit is finally moving

In the past month, we have finally seen a move up in credit spreads, from historic lows:

We are using corporate bonds single-B spreads as a proxy for leveraged loans here. The above is the ICE BofA single-B US High Yield Index Option-Adjusted Spread, which is currently sitting at 4.69%. Kindly note, in February 2025 this figure was 2.65%. The move up in spreads has been swift and violent.

While we are not at the highs seen in 2022, i.e. 500 bps and above, we are nearing that figure. What is shocking is the speed of the move, having happened in virtually a month, while the economic backdrop outside tariffs remains the same. Unemployment is still low, and GDP growth is still robust. The credit markets, however, cannot ignore the tariff impact and the associated uncertainty.

JFR has been walloped: both in NAV and market price

Firstly, let us look at how JFR has done in the past month:

When comparing JFR to a cohort of peers, we can notice that Invesco Senior Income Trust (VVR) is the outlier in terms of performance. We have a piece out on that name and the drivers, so if interested, you should read that. JFR's move is virtually in line with the Eaton Vance Floating Rate Income Trust (EFT). As a CEF, Pioneer Floating Rate Trust (PHD) did the best. We also introduced a non-leveraged component, namely Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN) for comparison purposes. BKLN is down a mere -3.73% here. Leverage delivers magnified returns, both on the upside and downside.

While JFR's move in not the worst, kindly keep in mind the CEF was already trading at a discount to NAV given its corporate action:

The rights offering moved the CEF to a slight discount to NAV, only to see that figure really widen out in the recent market move to close to -12%. This is the bottom of its historic range on a decade long look-back. We are of the opinion this is an extreme move given that economic fundamentals are still strong.

Let us look at the first chart from a NAV perspective now:

Now NAVs have not moved that much in the past month. In fact, the NAV moves for the CEFs are in line with the unleveraged ETF. Most of the price action has come from the significant widening of the discounts to NAV.

Is the discount to NAV justified? How about the yield?

From a distribution standpoint, approximately 82% of the yield is supported by cash-flow, while the rest is return of capital. Rates are undoubtedly going down, which will result in further pressure on distributions. Given the recent market turmoil, traders have now yet again increased the projected Fed Funds cuts for 2025 to an astounding five:

Is this correct? Will it happen? We do not know. Nobody knows, in fact. What we know for sure is that rates are moving down, and JFR will need to adjust its distribution. Let us be very conservative here and pencil-in a sustainable distribution after a 100 bps in Fed Funds cuts of roughly 10%, from the current 13.5%.

What we are looking at therefore is a CEF with a rough 10% yield (covered) after all said and done, an instrument which is trading at the bottom of the range in terms of discount when looking back ten years. Are defaults still low? You bet. Is the economy still doing ok? Absolutely.

Yes, the market is forward-looking, but right now, all possible bad news are priced in. And we include in the term 'bad news' the following: much lower Fed Funds, a higher default rate, higher unemployment. In our view, JFR is now slightly oversold, with a tariff normalization brining about a +5% move from the discount to NAV moving up and a slight NAV recovery.

While not apples for apples given some NAV slippage, also note that JFR's price is now close to its Covid levels:

Tactical trade versus outright buy and hold

We are of the opinion that JFR is set to bounce from oversold conditions, and just like we did for VVR, we are of the opinion that a tactical trade here can be done. We are targeting a +5% bounce from oversold conditions that embed an Armageddon market valuation. Some investors can tactically trade around a position given market metrics. We are one of them. We hold JFR but will trade around position size on this market move.

Conclusion

JFR is a leveraged loan fund. The name saw a rights offering earlier in the year which increased its cash position (a defensive stance), but has been creamed by the recent market action despite a sticky NAV. The CEF is now trading at a -12% discount to NAV, the bottom of its historic range. We are a tactical 'Buy' here for the name, aiming for a +5% bounce from deeply oversold conditions.

data.title: Voya Infrastructure, Industrials And Materials Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

The Fund seeks potential enhanced stability of returns over a market cycle through investments in global companies with potential to benefit from increased infrastructure spending, and by selling call options on select equity Indexes or exchange-traded funds.

Key takeaways

• During the quarter, the Fund benefited from strong stock selection, particularly within the United States and within the utilities, industrials, materials and information technology sectors.

• U.S. stocks remain attractive driven by post-election optimism, investment, productivity gains from deregulation, innovation and energy independence.

• Outside of the U.S., pockets of weakness in China and the eurozone have become more apparent.

Portfolio review

For the quarter, the Fund provided a total return of 3.94% on a net asset value basis, and a total return of the Voya Infrastructure Industrials and Materials Custom Index, returned 6.78%.

Equity portfolio

For the reporting period, the equity sleeve of the Fund outperformed its reference Index, the Voya Infrastructure Industrials and Materials Custom Index, due mainly to a positive impact from the core model where five of five pillars were positive. Stock selection effects were another contributor to performance. On the regional level portfolio holdings in the U.S. contributed the most while portfolio holdings in Japan were the largest detractors.

At the sector level stock selection in utilities, industrials, materials, and information technology had a positive impact on performance. Among the key contributors was an overweight position in Delta Air Lines, Inc. (DAL), Entergy Corp. (ETR) and F5, Inc (FFIV).

Conversely, stock selection in communication services and energy detracted from performance. At the stock level, the underweight positions in Honeywell International Inc. (HON), Boeing Co. (BA) and Williams Co, Inc (WMB).

Option portfolio

During the quarter, the Fund's option strategy had a positive impact on returns. The covered call strategy seeks to generate premiums and retain some potential for upside appreciation. The Fund implemented this strategy by typically writing call options on sector and regional exchange-traded funds (ETFs), the selection and allocation of which resulted from an optimization intended to closely track the Fund's reference Index. The strike prices of the options written were typically at or near the money, with expiration dates around one month at inception.

Current strategy and outlook

Global challenges and opportunities confront the markets in 2025. However, the U.S. remains attractive driven by post-election optimism, investment, productivity gains from deregulation, innovation and energy independence. Despite some softening, the U.S. labor market remains resilient. Inflation is expected to stay manageable, allowing the U.S. Federal Reserve to continue its gradual easing.

Outside the U.S., pockets of weakness are becoming more apparent, particularly in the eurozone and China.

The eurozone faces cooling services and ongoing manufacturing challenges, heightening economic uncertainty. In China, new policies aim to address economic sluggishness, especially in the property market, and boost investor confidence. However, the investment environment looks more promising in Japan, where deflation has been curbed and corporate reforms have made progress. Despite this, there has been relatively little foreign investment, creating an opportunity as more investors recognize the growth story.

Holdings detail

Companies mentioned in this report percentage of portfolio investments, as of 12/31/24: Delta Air Lines, Inc. 0.67%, Entergy Corp. 0.98%, F5, Inc. 0.80%, Honeywell International Inc. 0.36%, Boeing Co. 0.39% and Williams Co, Inc. 0.51; 0% indicates that the security is no longer in the portfolio. Portfolio holdings are subject to change daily.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Onity Group: Bottoml Lne Performance Will Close The Book Value Discount
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Onity Group (NYSE:ONIT) is trading at a 50% discount to its book value; this significant discount could be attributed to many factors, namely, a historically bloated balance sheet with near-term debt maturities, unimpressive bottom-line performance, and high capital-distractive acquisitions. Most of those concerns are behind us. The company has been profitable for nine consecutive quarters and delivering an impressive ROE on an adjusted basis. Management is committed to deleveraging the balance sheet and improving the bottom-line performance through hedging and revenue diversification; they have managed to reduce interest payments by $14 million, which will further improve earnings. I estimate in the medium term these improvements will make the stock attractive and close the book value discount.

Company background

Onity Group (formerly known as Ocwen Financial Corporation) is an independent mortgage servicer, unlike banks, which tend to service most of the mortgages they originate. Mortgage servicers like ONIT purchase Mortgage Service Rights [MSR] from financial institutions like Freddie and Fannie (OTCQB:FNMA) and Rithm Capital (RITM). Once these MSRs are purchased, the company handles the administrative work, i.e., makes payments to lenders, manages escrow accounts, and handles delinquencies. To generate revenue, Onity charges service fees for the MSRs they service.

Business segments

The service segment is only 85% of the company's revenue; Onity also has an origination side of the business, where they originate mortgage loans and then sell them for profit to other servicers. Onity also originates reverse mortgages, which it gets to service itself, mainly for hedging purposes. The origination segment is only responsible for 15% of Onity's revenue, yet it has been the fastest growing in 2024; origination grew by 33% to a volume of $29.7 billion in unpaid principal balance [UPB] vs. the overall industry growth of 17%. After loan origination and securitization, the company has an option to either sell the MSR and retain subservicing work or fully retain the mortgage rights with servicing. If the customers are satisfied with ONIT's services, they don't churn and instead originate more loans. ONIT has one of the best recapture rates, 1.6x, which is inline with the industry average.

Onity manages over $300 billion in UPB, $159 billion from subservicing, where the company only handles the administrative side of the mortgages, and $142.7 billion in owned servicing, where they also deal with payments, loan defaults, and delinquencies. Because there is no balance sheet risk with the subservice business, fees accrued are usually lower than fees accrued from the owned MSR service business; this helps to stabilize ONIT's revenue since owned service fees are sensitive to various factors like payment defaults and the Market Adjusted Value [MAV] of MSRs.

The company has been intentional in keeping a 50/50 mix of owned servicing and subserving to ensure that revenues are less sensitive to mortgage rates and to limit its advance obligations. If the company only did 100% sub-servicing, revenue growth would be limited by low subservicing fees; meanwhile, if it did 100% owned servicing, the company's balance sheet would be more exposed to external market forces like interest rates and loan defaults, and they would have to pay more advances as a result. So a 50/50 mix is financially optimal for risk reduction and revenue growth.

Mortgage rates

Onity's revenue varies depending on mortgage interest rates; when interest rates are high, it takes even longer for borrowers to repay their loans (UPB runoff rate decreases), which means mortgage servicers like Onity get to collect fees for a longer period of time. The reverse happens when interest rates decline. Since MSRs are valued as a basis point of UPB, a shrinking loan balance means a direct loss in servicing income. But the decrease in interest rates is good for loan origination; when interest rates are low, more customers are willing to take on mortgage loans. To circumvent the impact of interest rates, Onity keeps a 90%-110% hedge coverage ratio against the MSRs on their balance sheet; in comparison, Mr. Cooper Group (COOP) keeps a 75% hedge ratio.

The chart below illustrates that mortgage rates had gone up since 2021, but that they have started to show a slight decline from the year-end of 2023 to date. The Trump administration has made commitments to lower interest rates so that housing can be more affordable. Even though the future cannot be assured, we can make conservative estimates that we shouldn't expect significant rate hikes in the near term. Because of the hedge coverage ratio, I don't expect lower rates to impact Onity's revenue significantly.

2024 earnings performance

Income Statement (GAAP Metrics)

Onity's revenue decreased by 8% compared to 2023 revenue; this was due to a 12% decline in the servicing business and a 20% increase in other revenue sources, including the origination business. Operating and interest expenses increased by 6%, while pledged MSR expenses decreased by 47%. The company made $22.9 million in equity investment gains, which significantly contributed to its GAAP net profit of $34 million, a 47% decline vs. 2023. When looking at the income statement, we also see a $49.4 million loss from the extinguishment of debt; this is a non-cash restructuring charge as a result of the company refinancing its debt.

The restructuring charge in the 4th quarter of 2024 was $41 million and resulted in the GAAP loss of $3.63 per share, Q4 GAAP ROE of -25%, and 2024 GAAP ROE of 8%. Adjusting for the non-cash expenses, 2024 pretax income was $90 million, and ROE was 20%, exceeding company guidance by 8%. The company then increased its guidance for the full year of 2025 to the midpoint of 17% and further targeted a 10% YoY growth in overall revenue.

I find the guidance to be totally justified; the company did an amazing job in growing both their services and origination business. Onity added $25 billion in servicing UPB in Q4, a 70% increase YoY and the highest addition since Q2 of 2022. Onity grew owned MSR by $18 billion and subservicing by $7 billion in Q4.

Balance sheet

A majority of Onity's assets and liabilities are associated with their reverse mortgage business that it primarily used to hedge forward mortgages; about $11 billion worth of reverse mortgage loans are held in its balance sheet, and a complementary $10.9 billion of home equity conversion mortgage-backed securities (HMBS) is held on the liability side. The company has about $2.5 billion worth of MSR, an 8% increase from 2023, which offsets the 2024 sale of $15 billion worth of MSRs in UPB. There are $1.3 billion worth of origination loans held for sale, with a 90% increase YoY, and cash declined by 8% to $185 million.

Looking at the liabilities side, senior notes decreased by 18% to $487.4 million. The company's strategy in handling debt was to essentially push near-term maturities by a couple of years to reduce short-term illiquidity. They managed to eliminate $289 million of 7.9% senior notes due in 2026 and $285 million of 13% lien notes due in 2027 and replace them with $500 million of 9.9% 2029 senior notes. Through this debt restructuring, the company is estimated to have freed up about $14 million from interest expenses, which will further improve the bottom line. The company was able to increase shareholder equity by 21% to $493 million.

Quant ratings

Prior to their recent earnings report for Q4 2024, Seeking Alpha's quant rating rated ONIT a strong buy with a C+ for profitability and an A- on revisions. Post earnings, the stock initially got rated a hold and is now rated a sell, and the reason for that is because the company missed analysts estimates by a wide difference on GAAP EPS as a result of those restructuring charges I mentioned earlier. This led to a quant downgrade of D- for profitability and C+ on revisions. Obviously, the quant system is not factoring in the transient nature of non-cash expense impact on earnings. It's not just the quant system, but also the broader market has a bearish sentiment on this name because of the disappointing GAAP report.

Valuation

I believe the market is undervaluing this name and not paying attention to the improvements management has made in the recent quarters. The company trades at a book value of 0.5, while COOP, its closest competitor, trades above book value at 1.8.

On an adjusted basis, Onity has a higher ROE than its peers but continues to trade at a discount to book value and to that of its peers. Part of this reason is that the company is undercovered; the recent coverage article from Seeking Alpha dates back to 2023, and there is no article coverage on VIC. This article gives a good background on the name and the mortgage service industry as a whole.

Speculations for a sale

There have been rumors and speculations that management is considering selling Onity Group; these speculations are yet to be confirmed or refuted by Onity. According to Inside Mortgage Finance, the article further states that there's even of an offering book speculating to potential buyers. There is a likely chance that these rumors are true given that insiders own about 10% of the company; there's a direct financial benefit to the board if the company ever gets bought, especially looking at the premium price the company might sell for (assuming it sells at book value). Looking at how attractive Onity, it's not far-fetched to assume there is a potential buyer looking to place a bid. One such potential buyer could be Rithm Capital (RITM), one of Onity's largest clients, accounting for over $40 billion (14%) of Onity's UPB and 63% of delinquent loans.

Rithm Capital acquired Newrez in 2018; Newrez offer similar services to those offered by Onity, so merging both companies would be a perfect growth strategy for Rithm Capital while also strengthening its dominant position. However, given that not much has been said by Onity regarding the sale, it is safe to stay tuned even though I do see an upside potential for "buy the rumors and sell the news" types of investors.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I love management's growth strategy of ensuring the mix in owned MSRs servicing and subservicing and also maintaining a cautious hedge ratio as a protection for low interest rates. Diversifying revenue channels while hedging forward mortgage rate shocks shows that the company is committed to improving and sustaining its bottom line performance. Also, the company is showing effort in deleveraging its balance sheet, reducing interest payments, and pushing debt maturities by 3 years. The company currently doesn't pay dividends or have a share-repurchase program, but it's still an attractive buy at these prices; hence, I grant it a buy rating.

data.title: Semrush Holdings: Valuation Has Become Too Cheap
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about SEMrush Holdings (NYSE:SEMR) previously with a buy rating, as I was confident that SEMR would see growth accelerate back to the mid-20s. It has been a wild few weeks over the past two or three months since I updated in January. SEMR stock price surged to a peak of ~$19 (vs. my price target of $22) in early February before taking a nosedive all the way down to ~$8 today (at the time of writing). I believe the sell-off has nothing to do with the fundamentals and was largely market-driven, and since fundamentals have gotten better, I believe the risk-reward has gotten even more attractive. As such, I upgrade SEMR to a strong buy.

Growth outlook remains sound

Let me start with the key metrics that I monitor to evaluate SEMR’s growth outlook.

Firstly, SEMR enterprise sales motion is working, and it is doing much better than I expected, as inferred from the rate of winning deals. Here is a quote from the recent earnings prepared remarks: “We closed over 40 Enterprise SEO deals in December alone and more deals in Q4 than the rest of the year combined, ending 2024 with 144 Enterprise SEO customers with an average ARR per customer exceeding $60,000. We ended the year with $9 million of ARR (annual recurring revenue) from our new Enterprise SEO solution.” This level of traction suggests that SEMR saw accelerating momentum in deal flow/conversion rates.

Secondly, SEMR is still seeing very solid traction in up/cross-selling. Remember that this was one of the key growth catalysts I noted in my January update. The data is in the pudding: the number of accounts with >$50k ARR grew 82% y/y to 336, and customers with >$10k ARR grew 40% y/y to around 4,700. The implication here goes beyond the fact that SEMR makes more revenue. I believe it has a huge positive knock-on impact on SEMR’s growth potential because customers that spend more on SEMR have the tendency to spend more (i.e., carry higher expansion potential). My logic is that this is akin to a virtuous cycle. Customers that use more of SEMR are those that see great value (if not, why do they use more?), and the more they use, the better value they get, which leads to more expansion of use cases. You can also see this in average revenue per customer, which continued to grow at ~13% in 4Q24 (3rd quarter of >10% growth since 4Q22). This clearly signals a broader platform adoption and effective cross/up-selling.

Lastly, retention metrics remain healthy. While net revenue retention [NRR] dipped slightly to 106% in 4Q24, I see that as more of a blip than signs of deterioration, as management noted that it had already rebounded to 107% in January. One thing I should point out to readers is that NRR should not be evaluated in a vacuum. My view is that this 107% NRR actually tells us that the quality of SEMR’s customers has improved sequentially. Note that the total number of paying customers was flat at ~117k in 4Q24 and that NRR of business & agency customers was >120% (this is the area that SEMR is working hard to capture more), which offset the softness in freelancer and solopreneur (NRR < 100%). As such, while NRR was stable at 107%, at the underlying level, there was a very positive mix shift going on.

Hence, from a fundamental standpoint, I don’t see any structural weakness. In fact, I believe SEMR fundamentals have continued to improve, and the potential for growth acceleration has gone up.

AI Optimization Offering Is Another Growth Catalyst

The other major development that I think instills even more confidence that growth is set to accelerate is the formal rollout of SEMR’s AI Optimization [AIO] strategy. Currently, SEMR only has one product live at $99/month, and it plans to enter closed beta for an enterprise version in 1H25. While this is obviously still in the early innings and any revenue contribution is likely to be minimal in the near term, the AIO strategy excites me because of the adoption and monetization potential.

For those that are unaware, the general idea of AIO is that it helps customers to optimize visibility within AI-generated search responses. I believe this is going to be a major “battleground” for a lot of businesses as we transition from a Google SEO-led world into an AI-search-led world. In the past, in order to have sufficient digital reach/presence, one had to rely a lot on Google (since most people used it for discovery purposes). Times have changed. Increasingly, there are more people using AI to search (ChatGPT, for instance), and the way results are shown is very different from how Google ranks their results. As such, the timing of SEMR’s AIO rollout is a timely one. As search behavior evolves, I think there will be an increasing demand for solutions to ensure each organization’s content shows up in AI-generated results. Given that SEMR is already a platform that has all the data (internal data base of keywords and insights to improve visibility, etc.), I believe it is in a good position to compete for market share here.

Valuation is not demanding

SEMR valuation has been crushed to a level that, I think, is too cheap, and I see this as a very attractive entry point to invest in SEMR. The business fundamentals have continued to improve, making the growth acceleration outlook even better. While there may be near-term volatility due to the macro situation, investors with a longer-term investor duration will benefit, as I still expect revenue growth to accelerate back to 25% by FY27. SEMR: If we benchmark against early February, where SEMR traded at 5.5x forward revenue, the upside is extremely attractive.

Even if we assume SEMR growth decelerates continuously for the next two years to 10% in FY27 and the valuation multiple stays at this level (which I think is pricing in rapid growth deceleration), there is still ~11% upside.

Risk

There are risks that investors should be aware of. Firstly, as I said before, if we do go into a recession, regardless of the cause, SEMR growth is very unlikely to accelerate. Secondly, if there is an execution hiccup in continuing the up/cross-selling momentum, SEMR may not be able to offset the weakness seen in the lower-quality customers (freelancers and solopreneur segments). Lastly, AIO may turn out to be a flop, and this may limit the pace of growth acceleration.

Conclusion

I upgraded my rating to strong buy. Despite recent market volatility, SEMR’s fundamentals have gotten stronger—enterprise momentum is real, customer monetization is scaling, and AIO offers a compelling new growth lever. With valuation now reset to near trough levels, I believe SEMR now offers very attractive risk-reward.

data.title: Stocks Go Orbital On Trump's Tariff Timeout
data.description:
Wall Street jettisoned gravity today as President Trump called a 90-day armistice in his on-again, off-again trade war. A record 30 billion shares took flight in the best showing since 2008 and the Nasdaq’s second-best performance on record.

Level Change 4/9/25 (%)

+7.9 Dow

+12.2 Nasdaq

+12.1 Nasdaq 100

+9.5 S&P 500

+9.3 S&P 400

+8.8 S&P 600

The day began in a blue funk, courtesy of Beijing’s decision to lift tariffs on US goods to 84% from 34%. Talking heads groaned that a structural rebalance of global trade is underway, and not the sort likely to earn applause on either side of the Pacific.

Reports surfaced of companies raising prices to stay afloat amid the tariff squall. Analysts dutifully trimmed their S&P 500 targets. With Q1 earnings looming, Wall Street’s latest parlor game is guessing whether companies will even bother offering guidance. On a floor this slick, would you?

But the big overhang was the bond market, where analysts whispered about a “disorderly forced liquidation” - someone’s spreadsheet went up in smoke. Yields spiked as the so-called basis trade fell apart like a rental tux at closing time.

This arbitrage strategy involves shorting Treasury futures while buying the actual bonds (or the reverse), aiming to pocket the sliver of difference - the basis - when prices converge.

But when the spread moves the wrong way, traders don’t stroll to the exits; they stampede. Bonds get dumped, futures get bought back, and the whole trade flips from tidy to terrifying in a Trumpian minute. That’s what looked to be unfolding in the three days leading up to this morning.

On Monday and Tuesday, the 30-year Treasury notched its biggest yield surge since the pandemic began, a move that sent the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT +0.6%) tumbling 4.9% over the two days, a full-on faceplant by long-bond standards. Over on LinkedIn, Jim Bianco of Bianco Research dubbed it a “tell your grandchildren I was there” kind of day in the bond market.

Rumor has it that it wasn’t stocks, but imploding bonds, that forced Trump to tap the brakes on tariffs. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent denies this, of course. Reporters noted yields rising faster than a bad smell in a small elevator. They asked whether fears about a liquidity crisis and Treasuries losing their safe-haven status spurred Trump’s tactical timeout.

Officially, no. Unofficially, let’s just say the timing was exquisite.

“This was driven by the president’s strategy,” Bessent swore. “He and I had a long talk on Sunday, and this was his strategy all along.” On Fox Business, he offered further reassurance that these “deleveraging convulsions” happen now and then, no biggie.

Well, it was a biggie to potential home buyers, whose stock portfolios tanked as interest rates rose, a most unwelcome housewarming gift from the bond market.

But never mind the home buyers, the big takeaway is that the Trump Put exists after all, just lower than long imagined. Stocks can fend for themselves, but when the bond market raises its voice, Team Trump drops the act - as Bessent… denies. The plan all along? Sure thing, Scott. Plan?

Tonight, crack or pop something in celebration, just don’t get carried away. Universal 10% tariffs are still tugging at profits, inflation is all the Fed can talk about, US-China tension shows no signs of retreat (Trump just hiked China tariffs to 125%), and the chaos crew still holds the remote.

Volatility hasn’t left the building. It may have just stepped out for air.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Voya Global Advantage And Premium Opportunity Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

The Fund combines an actively-managed quantitative equity investment strategy with a call writing option strategy, seeking to create a diversified portfolio with enhanced total return potential and strong downside capture over a full market cycle.

Key takeaways

• During the quarter, the Fund benefited from the strong performance of its equity sleeve, particularly the operations pillar.

• U.S. stocks remain attractive driven by post-election optimism, investment, productivity gains from deregulation, innovation and energy independence.

• Outside of the U.S., pockets of weakness in China and the eurozone have become more apparent.

Portfolio review

For the quarter, the Voya Global Advantage and Premium Opportunity Fund provided a total return of 0.36% on a net asset value (NAV) basis and a total return of 0.31% on a market price -4.20%.

All five pillars within the model were positive, with the operations pillar performing the strongest. The fund's lower beta positioning was the second-largest contributor. Dividend yield and size were the largest detractors from performance.

On the regional level, portfolio holdings in the U.S. contributed and holdings in Japan detracted.

On the sector level, stock selection in the utilities, health care and industrials sectors contributed the most to results. Among the key individual stock contributors were the non-benchmark position in Unum Group (UNM), the overweight in Kinder Morgan Inc. (KMI) and a non-benchmark position in DT Midstream, Inc (DTM).

By contrast, stock selection in the communication services, financials and consumer discretionary sectors detracted. Among the key detractors were not owning JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), and Bank of America Corp. (BAC) as well as an overweight in Elevance Health, Inc (ELV).

Option portfolio

During the quarter, the Fund's option strategy had a positive impact on returns. The strategy seeks to generate premiums and retain some potential for upside appreciation. The Fund implements this strategy by typically writing call options on regional indices, the selection and allocation of which resulted from an optimization intended to track the reference Index of the Fund closely. The strike prices of the options written were typically out of the money or near the money with expiration dates around six weeks at inception.

Current strategy and outlook

Global challenges and opportunities confront the markets in 2025. However, the U.S. remains attractive driven by post-election optimism, investment, productivity gains from deregulation, innovation and energy independence. Despite some softening, the U.S. labor market remains resilient. Inflation is expected to stay manageable, allowing the U.S. Federal Reserve to continue its gradual easing.

Outside the U.S., pockets of weakness are becoming more apparent, particularly in the eurozone and China.

The eurozone faces cooling services and ongoing manufacturing challenges, heightening economic uncertainty. In China, new policies aim to address economic sluggishness, especially in the property market, and boost investor confidence. However, the investment environment looks more promising in Japan, where deflation has been curbed and corporate reforms have made progress. Despite this, there has been relatively little foreign investment, creating an opportunity as more investors recognize the growth story.

Holdings detail

Companies mentioned in this report percentage of Fund investments, as of 12/31/24: in Unum Group 0.53%, Kinder Morgan Inc. 0.70%, DT Midstream, Inc. 0.60%, JPMorgan Chase & Co. 0.00%, Bank of America Corp. 0.00% and Elevance Health, Inc. 0.74%; 0% indicates that the security is no longer in the portfolio. Portfolio holdings are subject to change daily.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: JPMorgan Earnings Preview: Tough Compares Meet Harder Capital Markets
data.description:
Now the world’s premier banking institution, with the banking world’s premier CEO, JPMorgan (NYSE:JPM) will report their Q1 ’25 financial results before the opening bell on Friday, April 11th, 2025.

Consensus estimates for the bank giant are expecting $4.61 in earnings per share (EPS) on $44.1 billion in revenue for expected y-o-y growth in EPS of exactly 0% (flat y-o-y) on -1% revenue growth.

The problem with JPM is history: 2023 and 2024 EPS growth was +23% and +15% respectively, while revenue growth in ’23 and ’24 grew 23% and +12 respectively. The bank – as good as it is – was punching above its weight class the last two years, supported by good capital markets a robust US economy.

It’s likely that 2025 will be a middling year for the bank giant. A lot of sell-side firms thought the IPO and M&A business, which was thought to have a lot of pent-up deals waiting to get done, seemed to fizzle in Q1 ’25. High expectations wound up meeting middling reality.

JPM’s CIB, which is the Corporate and Investment Bank segment, which represented 40% in net revenue and 47% of segment operating profits in Q4 ’24, might see their results return to the 8-quarter average of 33% and 34% respectively in 2025.

Valuation

With the drop in JPM’s stock price from $280 late in ’24 to $235 today (after the big rally), the P/E has compressed a little bit on JPM, as the EPS estimates haven’t changed much with the market turmoil.

At $235 per share, JPM is trading at 13x and 12x 2025 and 2026 EPS of $18.22 and $19.46, for expected y-o-y growth of 0% and 7% respectively.

JPM was always the priciest bank on a book and tangible book basis, so if those metrics haven’t changed much, with the drop in the stock book value and tangible book value are now 2x and 2.4x respectively.

With the drop in the stock to $235, JPM’s dividend yield has jumped over 2%.

In 2024, JPM’s ROTCE (return on tangible capital shareholders' equity) was 22% in Q1, 20% in Q2, 19% in Q3 ’24 and 21% in Q4 ’24. Those are stellar metrics for a bank the size of JPM.

I’m expecting JPM’s ROTCE to decline a little bit in 2025, as the various issues that have caused the market to selloff so far this year, wash through the remaining 3 quarters of the year. Morningstar has a targeted zone of 16-17% for JPM’s ROTCE, but the bank has done consistently better than that in years with a decent market tailwind.

Summary/conclusion

Given that the tariff turmoil didn’t really start until after Q2 ’25 started, the lion’s share of the capital markets disruption was not in Q1 ’25, hence JPM should probably have a decent or at the very least “inline” quarter with current estimates.

Q2 ’25 EPS and revenue estimates are looking for $4.56 in EPS on $43.9 bl in revenue, for expected y-o-y growth of +4% on -11% decline in revenue. JPM printed $49.87 billion in revenue in Q2 ’24, (if that actual net revenue print wasn’t fat-fingered with the spreadsheet entry). So Q2 ’25 guidance might be conservative as it is.

There are so many variables after the “tariff tiff” not the least of which is the bond market troubles, the drop in crude oil, etc., which surfaced this past 10 days, so there are a few reasons – the least of which being tough compares that JPM’s 2025 might be tougher than is expected.

You really have to admire Jamie Dimon though: it appears that a conversation he had with President Trump today, might have helped sway the 90-pause on the non-China tariffs.

Jamie has been pretty bearish since early 2022, when he proclaimed the “economic hurricane” was upon us. There were more bad recession calls by Wall Street in 2022, than I’ve ever seen in my time in the business, and that’s going on 30 years. Dimon is still the best CEO in the business, and one of the top 10 CEOs in the US today.

With so many banks expecting a recession since 2022, the benefit to this has been the larger banks have been adding to loan loss reserves probably earlier than needed (at least 2 years early) so if we do get a recession, the banks are better reserved today than if the onset of the recession was swift like in 2020.

Personally, I think JPM will look at guidance hard on Friday morning. Both for JPM, and for the rest of the S&P 500, there is little reason for CEOs to stretch for a home run this year.

JPM was one of clients' largest holdings as of 3/31/25, but I still think the bank has a “middling” year, as it navigates the political and economic waters.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Investing can and does involve the loss of principal even for short periods of time. All EPS and revenue estimates are sourced from LSEG. None of this information may be updated, and if updated may not be done in a timely fashion.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Cryptocurrencies: Bitcoin Sinks To 5-Month Low
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

This weekly update tracks some of the largest cryptocurrencies by market share: bitcoin (BTC-USD) and ether (ETH-USD). While both are considered to be high-risk when it comes to investing, the two have foundational differences that investors should know.

We've also included XRP (XRP-USD), as it was one of the largest cryptocurrencies when this series began. According to Wikipedia, a cryptocurrency is "a digital asset designed to work as a medium of exchange that uses cryptography to secure its transactions, to control the creation of additional units, and to verify the transfer of assets."

Bitcoin

Bitcoin was the world's first cryptocurrency and decentralized digital currency. The first bitcoin transaction occurred in early 2009 and has since grown worldwide to a mainstream financial asset. It is often considered volatile, as seen in our first chart, but one can argue that it is also resilient.

Bitcoin's closing price sunk to its lowest level in five months, dropping over 10% from last week. BTC is down ~19% year to date and ~28% below its record high from January 2025.

Ether

Ether is a cryptocurrency run on the Ethereum blockchain platform and was launched in July 2015. It has the second largest market share, despite being the newest of the three discussed in this article.

Ether's closing price plummeted to a two-year low, falling nearly 23% from last week. ETH is down ~56% year to date and is ~69% below its record high from November 2021.

XRP

XRP, which is owned by Ripple, was launched in 2012 and was one of the larger cryptocurrencies for some time until new coins joined the market.

XRP surged during the last few months of 2024 and the during the first month of 2025, however, it has since pulled back to levels last seen in November. XRP's is down ~23% year to date.

Bitcoin vs. Ether vs. XRP

An index has been created in order to chart these three cryptocurrencies together, considering their significantly different pricing histories. The chart tells us which cryptocurrency's price has changed the most since November 9, 2017. At one point or another, all three have been at the top. At the time of writing, bitcoin is in the lead.

On January 10th, 2024, the SEC approved spot bitcoin ETFs from a range of issuers such as Grayscale Bitcoin Trust ETF (GBTC), iShares Bitcoin Trust (IBIT), Fidelity Wise Origin Bitcoin Fund (FBTC), ARK 21Shares Bitcoin ETF (ARKB), Bitwise Bitcoin ETF (BITB), Coinshares Valkyrie Bitcoin Fund (BRRR). Here are some of the quick takeaways from the spot bitcoin ETF launch.

On July 23rd, 2024, a handful of spot ether ETFs launched from a range of issuers such as Grayscale Ethereum Trust (ETHE), Franklin Ethereum ETF (EZET), Bitwise Ethereum ETF (ETHW), iShares Ethereum Trust (ETHA), Fidelity Ethereum ETF (FETH).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Even Amid Trade War, Nintendo Set To Game On With Switch 2 And Strong IPs
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Nintendo Switch 2 will drop on June 5th, and given the strength of the company's intellectual properties (IPs), Nintendo (OTCPK:NTDOY) (OTCPK:NTDOF) should prove to be a winner, especially in the long run. Further, while the Switch 2 and its accompanying software will surely remain the centerpiece of the company's ambitions, emerging opportunities, including movies starring Nintendo's characters and licensing for theme parks, should bolster Nintendo's revenues. Ultimately, I believe Nintendo offers an attractive opportunity for investors, especially over the next 3 to 4 years as the Switch 2 lifecycle reaches its peak.

The unfolding trade war has already had a major impact on Nintendo, sending prices tumbling in recent days. While the risks of a recession and economic turmoil can't be ignored, the recent decline offers investors an opportunity to pick up Nintendo shares at a discount. A healthy balance sheet and plenty of cash will help Nintendo weather storms if needed, and with digital software sales, which as of now won't be subject to tariffs, being a major source of profits, Nintendo can chart a successful path to profitability even if the trade war continues.

The First Switch Brought Nintendo Back From the Brink

Before the first generation Switch hit the market over 8 years ago, some analysts and industry experts argued that Nintendo's days as a console maker could be numbered and that the company should shift into game development full-time. Then the Nintendo Switch landed, and despite some early skepticism, the hybrid console took off.

Since its release in March 2017, the Nintendo Switch has sold over 150 million units. As far as lifetime sales are concerned, the Switch trails only the PlayStation 2 and Nintendo’s own DS handheld console. Will the Switch 2 match the first generation's success? The future is uncertain, but I'd be surprised if the Switch 2 doesn't turn out to be quite successful in its own right.

With the first Switch, Nintendo managed to move 1.359 billion games. The next-generation Switch will surely revitalize sales, although there's no guarantee that Nintendo will meet high expectations. Still, the large Switch user base, coupled with the strength of Nintendo’s first-party IPs, provides plenty of reasons to be optimistic.

Nintendo's Approach to Development Bodes Well For the Bottom Line

Some developers have struggled with AAA games and their often massive budgets. While Nintendo invests a lot in game development, they've generally avoided keeping up with the Joneses with things like hardware specs and graphics, choosing instead to focus on gameplay. Nintendo also strives to minimize development time and emphasizes efficient development. This should help control costs and protect profit margins.

Another factor to watch is rising game prices. $60 games have been the standard for AAA games for many years now. However, Zelda: Tears of the Kingdom launched at $70 in 2023, and despite the higher price tag, the game still sold 21.55 (and counting) million units.

With the Switch 2, game prices are set to rise further. Donkey Kong Bananza will retail for $70, while Mario Kart World and Kirby and the Forgotten Land have been marked at $80. Let's (conservatively) assume that Nintendo will only mark up the first-party games it expects to be mega-blockbusters. During the first generation, Nintendo had nine games sell more than twenty million copies each. Combined, the top ten best-selling Nintendo games sold over 300 million copies. If Nintendo can achieve similar results (300 million sold) with the Switch 2 and a quarter of these top-selling games are sold at $70 and another quarter at $80 (rather than $60), it’d generate more than $2.2 billion in extra revenue.

If all or most of Nintendo's games end up costing between $70 and $80 and sales numbers match generation 1 sales numbers, Nintendo stands to gain even more. Admittedly, if Nintendo pushes too far, it may face consumer backlash, declining demand, and more people opting to buy second-hand. That said, when you consider that most Nintendo video games provide 10s of hours of entertainment, even an $80 game is relatively affordable entertainment (on a per-hour basis).

Outside of the first-party lineup, for many years, Nintendo struggled to attract external developers to their consoles. Smaller user bases, more limited hardware, and various other factors encouraged external developers to partner with Sony and/or Microsoft instead. Yet, the first Switch garnered strong third-party support, and the new, more robust hardware offered by the Switch 2 should also draw in strong third-party support. Nintendo earns a slice of the third-party sales, including 30% for games sold through its e-shop.

Key Financial Considerations

Nintendo is in solid financial shape, although some of the numbers may, especially at first glance, look rather dour. As shown below, in the first 3 quarters of FY2024, Nintendo’s net sales dropped by 31.4% (YOY) and its operating profit plummeted by 46.7% (YOY).

Console sales dropped 30.6% FY25/Q1-Q3 from a year prior, while software sales declined by 24.4%. However, with the first-generation Switch having already been on the market for so long and with a new console coming in, these declines are not surprising, nor do I find them concerning.

The mounting turmoil in markets, potential financial constraints, and other risks could be a grave threat for many companies in the months ahead. As of its last report, Nintendo had roughly $9.2 billion USD in cash and equivalents, $4.5 billion in short-term investments, and a total of $17.57 billion in current assets, versus $3.95 billion in total current liabilities on its balance sheet.

Meanwhile, Nintendo's total operating expenses (TTM) weigh in at 429,494,000 yen, or just over $2.9 billion USD. With so much cash on hand, Nintendo should be able to weather storms if needed and shouldn't struggle to keep the lights on. High total current assets should provide some cushion if things head south.

Admittedly, Nintendo's TTM P/E GAAP leaves a bit to desire at ~38 compared to ~18 for the industry median. This might seem concerning at first glance, but remember that sales of the 1st gen Switch and software have been tapering off. Nintendo's annual EPS declined from a peak of $.95 in 2021 to $.73 in 2024. The P/E should improve once Switch 2 hits the market and sales/revenues rebound. Recent share price declines also make Nintendo more attractive.

Console Lifecycle Impact on Shares

Video game consoles go through life cycles. Early adopters first pick up consoles when game selection is limited. From there, if the console gains traction, the user base steadily expands. Sometimes, growth is spurred by slashing prices (on both hardware and software), but Nintendo largely resisted price drops with the first-generation Switch.

Crucially, more games entering the market will provide consumers with more options and satisfy more diversified tastes, thus driving software sales. Even for console makers (rather than companies that focus solely on game development), software is a crucial source of revenue and typically offers higher margins than the consoles themselves.

You can see Nintendo's revenues below. The large upward peak observed between 2005 and 2010 was due to the peaking Wii console lifecycle. Later in console lifecycles, revenues may cool, especially as hardware sales decline. In 2024, Nintendo also saw software sales drop 24.4% (per the Financial Results Explanatory Material 3rd Quarter of Fiscal Year Ending March 2025 posted above).

[object HTMLElement]

[object HTMLElement]

Nintendo's net income, as shown above, did decline after peaking during the COVID-19 pandemic. This should come as no surprise, and if anything, net income remained quite strong for an aging console. Later in a console's lifespan, producing hardware and even software should become cheaper, allowing Nintendo to produce more profits off of shrinking revenue. This is observed when looking at Nintendo's annual profit margin.

[object HTMLElement]

Below, you’ll notice four sharp upward swings in Nintendo’s stock price. The first, occurring throughout 2007 and into 2008, coincided with the release and smash success of the Wii gaming system. Nintendo’s stocks slid in 2008, with the Great Recession likely contributing, and then bottomed after Nintendo launched the ill-fated Wii U in late 2012. NTDOY went on an upswing starting in 2017, when the Nintendo Switch was launched and enjoyed considerable success.

The stock spiked again in 2020 and 2021 amid the COVID-19 pandemic, which resulted in increased console adoption and shortages. Following the Switch 2 announcement, Nintendo once again enjoyed an upswing, although stocks have since cooled amid global trade tensions.

The past cannot guarantee the future, but I expect Nintendo’s upswing to generally continue through the rest of the year and into 2026, assuming the global economy avoids falling into a recession. However, the road may be bumpy, especially if trade tensions continue to pace or escalate. Also, Nintendo did experience dips during the first generation Switch lifecycle. Among other things, the initial and somewhat surprising success of the Switch may have led to a bit of exuberance in the markets.

Just looking at share prices in the past and the release of new console generations, one might conclude that even if Nintendo’s share prices rise over the next few years, they will inevitably cool as the Switch 2 reaches the middle of its lifespan. This is a possibility. However, Nintendo’s success has rarely been sustained from one console generation to the next and the company made several missteps over the years, resulting in a sort of boom-and-bust cycle. Going forward, I believe that Nintendo can avoid this boom-and-bust cycle by sticking with what they know works, chiefly including the Nintendo Switch's hybrid home/portable nature.

Nintendo Branching Into Other Forms of Entertainment

Nintendo’s cast of characters includes plenty of big names, including Mario, Zelda, Link, Donkey Kong, and Yoshi. Nintendo is leveraging these assets outside of video games, and with the right moves, the company may be able to establish healthy revenue streams from non-gaming industries.

The Super Mario Bros. movie (2023) pulled in $1.36 billion at the box office on a production budget of just $100 million. A live-action Zelda movie trilogy is rumored to be in the works, with Nintendo collaborating with Sony to bring it to the screen. Still, movies frequently flop and Nintendo may eventually suffer some silver screen stumbles.

While producing successful movies is no easy task, external partners can bring in the necessary talent and cover part of the funding. Universal footed half the bill of the 2023 Mario movie, for example, splitting risks.

The Trade War Will Complicate Things

Let’s acknowledge the elephant in the factory: the ongoing global trade tensions will impact many companies. With Japan currently facing 24% tariffs and Vietnam facing 46% tariffs (Nintendo previously shifted some Switch production from China to Vietnam owing to China-US trade tensions), Nintendo may be forced to mark up the Switch in the United States or eat the loss.

While Microsoft and Sony have often sold their consoles at a loss, Nintendo tries to turn at least some profit on hardware. Yet, even if Nintendo is forced to sell the Switch 2 hardware at a loss, it will still have a viable path to profitability. It’s crucial to note that much of Nintendo’s profits come from games, not the consoles themselves.

Further, physical games may be impacted by tariffs, but for now, games sold through Nintendo's e-shop won't be. With the e-shop, Nintendo won't need to split revenue with brick-and-mortar stores, which typically get 30% of the sale.

Other Risks Investors Should Consider

The Switch 2 could fall short of expectations, and Nintendo’s fate is closely tied to its consoles. Even a minor stumble could snowball. Of course, if a recession hits, Nintendo’s stock will likely be depressed. Video games might be one of the first things families and individuals on tight budgets cut.

The gaming industry has faced headwinds in recent months, with many developers cutting staff. Nintendo has seen sales and revenues cool as well, but this primarily seems to be due to the upcoming release of the Switch 2.

Reverse DCF Model

Based on current prices, free cash flows, a WACC set at 10%, an 8-year growth stage (equal to an 8-year lifecycle for the Switch 2, in line with the Switch 1), and an 8-year terminal stage set at 3.5%, in line with recent global GDP growth, Nintendo will need to grow at 16.32% according to the reverse DCF model. The terms here are rather conservative, with many analysts putting Nintendo's WACC at 6-8%, and the 3.5% terminal stage likely underestimating performance. Simply dropping the WACC to 7% lowers the growth rate to 12.12%.

Nintendo's historical free cash flow growth varied substantially owing to the console lifecycle and COVID-19. The company's free cash flow was reported at $5.7 billion on 03/31/21 before dropping to $2.5 billion on 03/31/22, for example. Still, over the Switch 2's console lifespan, 16.32% should be relatively easy to obtain, and Nintendo can and, I believe, will exceed this performance, which should lead to strong stock performance.

Takeaway: Nintendo Strikes Me as a Long-Term Winner

Especially in the long run, Nintendo strikes me as a solid stock to buy and hold. The upcoming Switch 2 should enjoy plenty of success. That said, unless the Switch 2 exceeds expectations, gains this year could be subdued as the market may have already priced in expectations. Regardless, I expect to see Nintendo's stock rise until at least the midpoint of the Switch 2's lifecycle (about 3–4 years).

Currently, Seeking Alpha’s Wall Street rating summary has one rating for NTDOY with a target price set at $25.40, which is optimistic but not unrealistic. I expect Nintendo's stock to trend upward through the rest of the year, reaching at least the low $20s, and in 2026, sustained momentum should push shares past $25. I would not be surprised if my views turned out to be conservative.

Every investment carries risks, and that is certainly true for Nintendo. The Switch 2 might flop. Or, the trade war could deepen, and Nintendo may find itself in a hard-to-sustain position. Still, Nintendo strikes me as a relatively safe investment, but also one with great upside.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
